data.title: My 10%+ Yield Cocktail Of 2026: 1 CEF, 1 BDC And 1 Covered Call ETF
data.description:
My strategy for 2026 remains unchanged from what I had established on day 1 when I started to invest for passive income and smart compounding.

Even though my gut feeling tells me that we are quite close to a steep correction due to the record-highs and 3-years of a strong bull-run, sitting on sidelines would be probably more dangerous than remaining invested in the market.

The key danger or risk is associated with an opportunity cost of not capturing the necessary cash flows that are required for income compounding.

As investors we have to balance between capital/value preservation and meeting our investment goals, which, in this particular environment, has indeed become a more difficult task. Not because of the lack of opportunity set to generate returns, but mostly due to elevated risks.

However, the reason why I can easily continue to put fresh capital at work into market amid this challenging backdrop is related to my overall philosophy of what 'capital/value preservation' means. For me it is not about avoiding drawdowns or lagging behind, say, the S&P 500 (SPY). Instead, it is about the following two things:

  1. Making sure that in the long-run the underlying principal remains stable and, preferably, grows at an inflation rate.
  2. Avoiding income cuts at all costs (including a situation when the dividends get funded at the expense of the underlying asset base).

In other words, I couldn't care less if my high-quality income-producing assets experience negative share price reactions due to, for example, an AI-driven sell-off. Actually, such situations are more than welcomed, because they provide an opportunity for better (higher yielding) reinvestments.

The secret sauce, obviously, lies in finding high-yielding assets, which can keep producing rich dividends in a sustainable fashion across different cycles.

3 key 'ingredients'

My Top 3 protected high-yield choices for 2026 are:

  • PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO)
  • Fidus Investment (NASDAQ:FDUS)
  • NEOS Gold High Income ETF (BATS:IAUI)

Let me explain each of them.

#1 CEF - PDO

PDO is a closed end fund, managed by PIMCO. It invests in above-average risk bonds and mortgage backed securities in a diversified fashion - the exposures are spread across many individual securities and duration profiles. Just as most PIMCO vehicles and closed end funds, PDO utilizes external leverage to enhance the underlying yield (as of now, the leverage accounts for ~33% of the total portfolio).

The investment case of PDO is primarily linked to its high-yielding current income streams, which currently offer 11% in monthly distribution. As elaborated above, the main thing to check in such instances is whether these dividends are safe and funded by sustainable sources, instead of, for example, "return on capital" component.

So, if we look at the section 19 notices of PDO, we will see that this CEF is one of the most sustainable ones in the overall PIMCO universe (from the dividend coverage perspective). Namely, on a YTD basis, PDO has covered its dividends by 103% the dividend, meaning that no return of capital or asset sales have taken place to keep the current income streams alive.

Now, if we speak about the net investment income prospects, we have to definitely pay an attention to the fact that almost 33% of PDO's asset base is fueled by short-term borrowings (i.e., reverse repo agreements). The cost of debt for these reverse repo agreements is directly correlated to the base rate levels, which is where the Fed has made several cuts this year and the expectation is that going forward there will be some additional cuts. The savings on interest expense front should definitely help PDO drive up the net investment income generation, which, in turn, should support the dividend coverage even further.

Finally, almost 40% of PDO's assets are tied to the 0–3 year maturity segment, which is driven by dynamics that are associated with the front-end of curve. The essence here is this: the lower the base rates, the higher the value of fixed income securities, which are located in the 0-3 year segment. While it is not something that will have any meaningful impact for the dividend investor, it is for sure positive for PDO's price and value stability.

#2 BDC - FDUS

FDUS in one of my favorite BDCs out there. Currently, it yields 11.4%, while offering a strong total return case for 2026 despite challenging backdrop that many BDCs face. As I have articulated in many of my previous BDC-related articles, the sector earnings are under pressure due to lower base rates and tighter spreads, which do not bode well for dividends.

However, FDUS is a rare exception, which is largely protected from these headwinds and, arguably, has the necessary value-creation engine to generate incremental value (price and durable income returns).

FDUS operates in lower middle market lending space, where the spread compression is not a significant challenge due to much lower competition (scale constraint for large private credit entrants).

FDUS has also one of the safest balance sheets in the BDC world (debt to equity of 0.7x, while the target level is usually around 1.1x). This spare capacity here is supportive for portfolio growth and thus incremental NII per share generation, which should at least partially offset the interest rate pressures.

Furthermore, each quarter FDUS has the luxury to retain surplus NII per share generation that could also be directed towards equity-funded growth. As of Q3, 2025 FDUS had 116% of a base dividend coverage level, which is nicely above the sector average of 100%.

Finally, and this is, in my view, a real game-changer, FDUS carries significant equity investment stakes in high-quality business (already dividend paying). And, as we know, equity-like returns are negatively correlated with the level of interest rates, as opposed to the pure-play private credit loan investments, where the yield decline in tandem with SOFR reductions. For example, as of Q3, 2025, the equity investment value represented 12% of the total portfolio with the current appreciation being 154% over cost (an indicative of well-performing equity investments, which have stronger odds for being successfully monetized).

#3 covered call ETF - IAUI

My final ingredient to 2026 high-yield cocktail is covered call ETF - IAUI. While at its core IAUI is similar to most of its peers such as the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) and the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI), the key difference lies in the underlying index exposure.

For JEPI and QQQI we are talking about the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), respectively. IAUI is, however, based on physical gold (GLD) on which a covered call overlay program is implemented. Exactly this aspect changes the entire game dramatically.

The core principles remains the same:

  • The written calls options against gold allow to receive option premium, which support attractive dividends (IAUI yields close to 12% now if we annualize the recently paid dividend amount - $0.5706 per share).
  • The calls also come with their disadvantages, where the participation in the underlaying's upside move is capped by the written call option strike values.
  • The downside remains almost fully open with a slight offset stemming from the option premiums.

The main risks are: 1) the opportunity cost of missing the upside and 2) downside if gold plunges. The former should not be a meaningful concern for income-oriented investors (again, alpha is not that important), but the latter is indeed something to understand deeper.

So, we have to ask ourselves a question whether we really believe that the gold will experience a significant plunge, or a decline that would be more pronounced than for QQQ or SPY, which are richly priced indices.

In my view, having a gold exposure in portfolio could come in handy in the case of further dollar depreciation, stubbornly high inflation, AI-driven sell-off and potentially damaged Fed independence (and I'm not even speaking about the various geopolitical risks that we have - all of which are inherently supportive for gold). In a nutshell, I do not view gold as a downside risk, but rather as a timely hedge, which is unlikely to lose value in a notable fashion, while we have so many factors supporting its 'safe haven' status.

data.title: GitLab: Downgrade To Hold On Near-Term Weakness
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to GitLab Inc. (GTLB) in September, with my key thesis being that growth was showing signs of re-acceleration, and key indicators have shown that GTLB is benefiting from the AI tailwind. My thesis still holds at a structural level. AI is increasing code volume and complexity, which supports long-term demand for DevSecOps. However, near-term growth has clearly decelerated. This dynamic may lead to valuation going lower before recovering. Hence, I downgrade to hold for the near term.

AI

For a quick recap, I discussed the GTLB core AI bear case in September. The idea was that AI would shrink developer seats, and with that, it would reduce GTLB platform demand. My stance was that AI wouldn't replace developers; instead, it should increase the volume and speed of code they produce. And when we layer in the complexity element (more code means more complexity), that should drive more needs for DevSecOps.

We had the Q2 2026 data to back my claim, and the recent numbers gave even more conviction that AI is not disrupting the GTLB growth runway. Specifically, the metric to track here is CI/CD pipeline deployments, which grew by >35% y/y. And this is important because it best reflects underlying demand and usage. Like I said before, AI helps developers to generate code at speeds that were not possible in the past. But with that speed, it comes with more bugs and security gaps (i.e., complexity), which happens because humans are not precise like machines. Here is where CI/CD comes into play. It is effectively the machine that helps to catch those errors and make sure the code is suitable for release. So, when we think about the sheer magnitude of AI-assisted code coming into the system, companies simply don't have the confidence to release them unless they're checked, and they do not have the manpower to check everything with precision. This is why I think GTLB will continue to see great demand ahead.

Monetization

GTLB's monetization story is also an attractive aspect of the stock's narrative, and I think there is a visible catalyst ahead that could help GTLB accelerate growth. In September, I pointed to GTLB's Ultimate tier adoption as evidence that GTLB is executing its "land and expand" nicely. And I believe we may see a surge in adoption because of the Duo Agent Platform.

To understand why this matters, I need to make clear the difference because AI assistants and AI agents. So far, most of the AI coding tools in the market are really AI assistants (things like GitHub Copilot). They basically help a developer write code faster, but a human still drives the overall workflow. Make no mistake, it is still a great product. But the Duo Agent Platform is a big step forward. You can think of these agents as independent employees that are able to triage issues, find ways to fix a complex problem, and merge changes on their own. This is a massive difference, as it essentially removes the "human" element from the workflow. In other words, developers now have more time to work on more important issues.

You can also think of this as GTLB effectively helping companies to generate more value per developer (now that they can focus on important stuff and probably work on more projects). This value creation, then, justifies GTLB to "raise prices." But GTLB did this in a smart way. They are adopting a hybrid structure with customers continuing to pay subscriptions for human seats, but agent activity will be monetized based on consumption (you pay for what the agents actually do, not how many people are logged in). This way, GTLB still gets the baseline recurring revenue and benefits from higher AI adoption (which also helps in protecting gross margin since revenue is aligned more directly with the compute cost of AI).

The latter point on monetization by consumption also helps to dismiss the bear case that GTLB is facing slowing customer adds. Since monetization is now based on consumption, that sort of decouples overall growth with the customer-adds metric. If a customer runs a leaner engineering team but leans heavily on agents to handle the work of multiple junior developers, GTLB still gets paid through usage.

Growth deceleration and air pocket

The two sections above are reasons why I think the GTLB long-term AI thesis still holds. However, I have become really worried about the near-term numbers. Recall my previous buy rating was based on GTLB showing growth re-acceleration. That did not happen. In fact, the data is now pointing the other way. Revenue growth slowed to 25% y/y in Q3 2026, and the implied outlook for Q4 2026 points to a further deceleration to ~19% y/y. Growth is what matters for GTLB (despite all the AI and monetization tailwinds), because it shows whether those positive elements are shaping up. We also know the market cares about growth because GTLB's forward revenue multiple aligns with the direction of expected revenue growth. When growth decelerates, valuation comes down.

Moreover, there are red flags appearing in GTLB's operating metrics that make me worried growth could continue to decelerate for longer than expected. As much as I like to think Duo Agent Platform would help GTLB better monetize (note: as of the Q3 report, this platform was not generally available yet), the latest data shows that GTLB is failing to do that. Net Dollar Retention [NDR] fell to 119%, down 200 bps sequentially, marking the eighth consecutive quarter where the metric has either declined or gone sideways. If in the next quarter we don't see any improvement, it tells me one of two things: (1) the Duo Agent Platform is not working as well as it should; and/or (2) there is something wrong with the go-to-market strategy. To be fair, 119% is still healthy (customers are still spending 19% more every year), but the direction of travel is what matters here. If this continues to go down at the same pace, we are looking at this going to <110% in a couple of quarters. That is a ~900 bps deceleration in overall growth.

Another reason why I am worried about the near term is because of the headwind GTLB will face in FY2027. For the past two years, reported growth benefited from a Premium tier price increase, which went from $19 to $29 (52% increase). That pricing tailwind is now gone (FY2027 will fully lap this). Although this has nothing to do with the fundamentals, it is really an optical y/y tough comp base issue, but the headline numbers are not going to be nice. Without that pricing tailwind, GTLB has to rely on pure volume growth just to support the current growth rate, which, in this case, is also not helpful, knowing that net new customer adds fell to just 956 in the quarter, the lowest level in four years. We are also unsure when the consumption-based pricing from agents will show up in a meaningful way. In the worst case, we could see a growth air pocket, which may spook the market given the focus on growth.

Valuation

Referring to the valuation chart above, I am worried that near-term growth deceleration will put more downward pressure on valuation. At ~4.7x, this is the lowest multiple GTLB has ever traded over the past 3 years. If we look at other platform/infrastructure peers with slower growth, like Tenable Holdings (TENB), which has high-single-digit growth rates and trades at 2.8x forward revenue, we could make a case that GTLB could trade at that level. If NDR drops to <110% and adds on the air pocket I mentioned, growth could drop close to high-single-digits (remember Q4 2026 revenue is guided for 19% growth).

Conclusion

I remain positive on GTLB's long-term outlook, particularly the tailwind from more AI usage by developers and the monetization optionality from agent adoption. That said, timing matters. Growth has slowed, NDR is declining, and the pricing tailwind is rolling off. Until there is clear evidence that organic growth can stabilize and re-accelerate, I believe GTLB deserves a more neutral view.

data.title: 2026 Market Outlook - A Perfect Storm Is Brewing
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last 5 days have shown how turbulent investors’ sentiments are in current market conditions, reflective of the whipsawed narrative in CY25.

Sentiment has radically shifted from last week, when the AI bubble debate ushered in the need for preparedness for a steep correction, to this week, when investors have sharply pivoted towards expecting a Santa Claus rally.

CY25 is on track for another year of strong double-digit gains after excitement around AI fueled robust gains in CY23 and CY24. But CY25’s gains came after months of turbulence stemming from sentiment souring over AI optimism coupled with global trade tensions, liquidity concerns, rising debt levels, and margin pressures.

In my opinion, which I detail out below, the turbulent market performance this year will set the base for a sense of realism in CY26 after years of optimism around AI. Valuation levels strongly suggest a deep pullback coming early next year before markets set course to record further all-time highs.

I continue to remain bullish on markets in general and have attached my PT for the S&P 500 below.

Recapping 2025’s Performance This Year

In 2025, AI once again dominated the narratives, but this time the sentiment around AI was clouded by a few headwinds. The convergence of these macro headwinds stirred up a debate that an ‘impending’ AI bubble was widely expected to burst any time this year, souring the optimism on the AI trade.

Nvidia (NVDA), the face of the AI industrial revolution, faced tough questions very early on this year when DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) Jan ‘25 launch rattled the AI spending outlook. AI spending eventually grew robustly and is expected to remain elevated at +50% growth this year too. I continue to believe that the AI rally still has legs, like I explained a month ago. However, the markets will likely attempt to be a lot more pragmatic, realistic, and rational as they turn the page towards CY26 with a keen eye out for macro headwinds.

We got a glimpse of that in CY25 when tech sectors, as seen below, did perform either at par with or above the S&P 500 (SP500). But these performances were muted as compared to the strong display in CY24. Global M2 liquidity did face some pressure, which may have put some curbs on the enthusiasm for growth stocks and AI. This can also be seen by the performance of Bitcoin (BTC-USD), which scored 124% in CY25 but is so far underperforming markets.

Global liquidity was pressured by the rapidly evolving situation around global trade policies, tariffs, and geopolitical tensions this year. On top of that, a stable-to-falling interest rate outlook created the perfect set of trigger points for global portfolios to diversify their capital away from AI and towards metals this year, as seen below.

What does this indicate as markets look towards CY26?

2026 Outlook: Mean Reversion To Fundamentals

If investors observe the performance above and compare it to the macro headwinds that I pointed out earlier, they will notice just one defining conclusion—that metals are still outperforming, and most of the macro headwinds (trade uncertainties, geopolitical tensions, etc.) are still persisting.

The AI trade still has legs, but investors are getting even more realistic about fundamental metrics like margins, cash flows, ROIC, etc., which will invite incrementally higher scrutiny in CY26. Markets are keenly watching if companies in the S&P 500 are reaching the ceiling in their margins, especially at a time when a large portion of free cash is being spent on capex, leading to a divergence in gross margins and free cash margins.

Markets may revert back to concerns about the AI bubble bursting next year, but I believe the real scrutiny will be on margins. That’s mainly because the rapid ascent in commodities in general (metals and materials, for example) will put pressure on gross margins due to increases in COGS and BOM (bills of materials).

Within AI as well, the cost of memory has surged this year, putting pressure on the margins of many AI players. Memory usually accounts for “20%-25% of the bill of materials” of AI servers, which can put immense pressure on the unit economics of buying and operating AI servers as well as running ML models on these servers. Therefore, I do expect gross margins to become an extremely crucial top-line component in the market's evaluation of winners and losers in CY26 as commodity prices rise across the board.

Also, heading into CY26, markets are starting to look rich, with the S&P 500’s forward PE hitting upper limits. As it now stands, the midpoint of consensus estimates puts the S&P 500’s CY26 earnings forecast at ~$310 per share, up ~14% versus CY25. At the same time, markets are trading at relatively the same forward earnings multiple they were trading at last year, as shown below.

In my November 22nd note on the S&P 500, I explained why a forward PE of ~23.5x is justified, an earnings multiple I still believe is justified today. This implies scope for ~8%, pointing me towards my target of 7500.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

While I do estimate the S&P 500 to reach ~7500 next year, the trajectory to this level can be shaky. One major uncertainty in 2026 will be the volatility coming from the US Federal Reserve.

While incumbent Fed chair Jerome Powell’s term ends in May, the US administration is “expected to announce a successor early in the new year.” A rising disconnect between the monetary point of view and federal point of view on interest rates, the transition in the Fed’s top post, and a complex structure at the Fed board will raise the uncertainty early next year, adding one more layer of macro headwinds to the market.

So far this year interest rates (US10Y) have remained relatively stable, but the uncertainty in the US Fed can spill over and distort the interest rate outlook in H1 CY26.

The persistence of all these headwinds—trade uncertainty, geo tensions, and volatility at the US Fed—poses a threat to the S&P 500 earnings multiple, which is getting rich and difficult to justify. But since the overall long-term outlook is strong, any pullback/correction will be a gift for investors to buy into.

Takeaway

While I do expect volatility to disturb markets in H1, I expect markets to eventually find middle ground and rally towards creating new highs next year. Until uncertainties linger, markets will have to weather the storm of expectations and macro volatility, but these pullbacks will present investors with perfect buying conditions since the long-term outlook remains strong.

I am bullish on the markets in general while keeping an eye out for uncertainties and have updated my PT for the S&P 500 accordingly.

data.title: 2026 Market Outlook: Solid Upside Ahead
data.description:
Article Thesis

While there are uncertainties and risks for the market in the upcoming year, there are also growth drivers, and there's potential for positive surprises. In this article, I'll lay out the risks and opportunities for the broad market, and will show why I believe that there's a good chance of solid returns over the next twelve months.

Where We Are At The End Of 2025

Markets were very volatile this year, but overall, 2025 was still a strong year:

Year-to-date, at the time of writing, the S&P 500 (SP500)(SPY) returned 17%, with the Dow Jones Index (DIA) returning 15%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) returned an even better 21% over the same time frame. This was possible despite a range of negatives that materialized throughout the year:

- Tariffs frightened investors, but as it turned out, those were less impactful than many thought in spring, when markets experienced a violent sell-off.

- Geopolitical tensions remained high in several parts of the world, with the Russia-Ukraine war still going on and with the Iran-Israel conflict escalating in the summer of 2025. Towards the end of 2025, tensions between the US and Venezuela are rising, which could lead to escalation down the road.

- Some jobs reports were worse than expected, which includes a big 900,000 jobs downward revision earlier this year.

And yet, despite these negatives, markets did very well this year, continuing their strong record seen since the pandemic, with the S&P 500's five-year return now standing at 98%, or around 15% per year [CAGR].

Outlook For 2026

Looking into next year, let's start with the positives that should be beneficial for equity returns in 2026.

Federal Reserve

While there is never any certainty when it comes to what the Federal Reserve will do when it comes to interest rates, it seems likely that we will see some interest rate reductions next year. Inflation is more tame than many thought for now, and with the job market not being overly strong, FOMC board members could be inclined to ease further. The Federal Reserve also, in December, stated that it would start buying bonds again, which is another form of easing that should be beneficial for equity markets. Looking at the CME's Fedwatch tool, we see that markets are seeing a high likelihood of interest rates falling to 275-300 or 300-325 by the end of 2026, versus 350-375 base points today. This would mean a reduction of 50 or 75 base points over the coming twelve months. While still significantly ahead of the interest rates we saw during the peak of the pandemic, a 0.5% to 0.75% reduction in interest rates, combined with the Fed's bond buying, should make investors more eager to buy equities, I believe.

Economic growth

Economic growth doesn't translate into equity returns on a 1:1 basis, but the two are correlated. When the economy is doing well, consumers are happy and eager to spend, investors are optimistic, and so on.

For 2026, the US economy is forecasted to grow significantly faster than many other developed economies. Goldman Sachs (GS), for example, sees a US GDP growth rate of 2.6% next year, which would be ahead of the forecasted 2025 growth rate of around 2%, and which should be better than growth in most EU countries, Japan, South Korea, Canada, and so on. While a 2.6% growth rate isn't thrilling, and below the expected growth for countries such as China or India, a 2.6% growth rate would still be a good result for an advanced economy like the US. GDP growth figures are inflation-adjusted, so a mid-2% growth forecast suggests real and significant progress.

Artificial Intelligence potential

While I do not believe that every hyped-up AI stock is a good investment – some AI picks seem overly expensive – Artificial Intelligence clearly is a major theme that influences all kinds of businesses and industries. With more and more companies using AI across their operations, in research, and so on, we could see AI-driven efficiency gains in 2026 and beyond. I don't think there will be a huge step-up in productivity and efficiency, but there should be gradual improvements over time – and that should result in some margin tailwinds for many companies, I believe. Margins don't have to explode upwards for there to be a meaningful impact on earnings growth –when a company with a 10% net margin grows its margin by just 30 base points, that alone makes for a 3% earnings increase, without any business expansion, revenue growth, etc.

Potential for easing tensions

While far from a certainty, there is potential for geopolitical tensions to ease. A peace deal between Russia and Ukraine doesn't exist yet, but talks could result in such a deal over the next couple of months. If that were to happen, consumers around the world would likely be happy about that, and sentiment would, I believe, improve, while trade flows could also benefit if a deal is made. The ongoing tensions between the US and China may also ease, which would be positive for US companies that are active in China and which could benefit both countries' GDP growth.

Risks in 2026

Of course, there are also risk factors to consider. Apart from black swan events that aren't predictable, some risks to keep an eye on include geopolitics and inflation. While we could see positive geopolitical developments in 2026, the reverse also holds true –tensions in the Middle East could rise again, for example, while current Russia-Ukraine talks may fail. Terrorism, potential trade issues, etc., are similar risks to keep an eye on. Inflation numbers have been better than many thought in November, but there is no guarantee that this will hold true for the future. If inflation were to re-accelerate – I think this is not very likely, but it's not impossible – then the Federal Reserve would likely be less dovish, and consumer sentiment could be negatively impacted by high inflation numbers as well.

Where Will The S&P 500 End The Year 2026?

For the current year, analysts are forecasting that the S&P 500 members will earn $275 per "share" of the S&P 500. For 2026, analysts are predicting earnings growth of around 15%, per this source – an unusually high growth rate compared to the past, driven in part by strong expected profit growth for companies such as NVIDIA (NVDA) that have a high index weight. If that happens, the S&P 500 profit per share would climb to around $316. Today, with an index level of around 6,880, the S&P 500 is trading for roughly 25x 2025's earnings. Looking at how the index was, on average, valued in the past, this is a rather high valuation. One can make a case that a higher-than-average valuation is justified, as expected earnings growth is strong while interest rates are currently declining, but I still think that it's reasonable to assume that valuations will come down a bit going forward.

I thus base my 2026 S&P 500 target on an earnings multiple of 23, which is down compared to current levels. I will also assume that earnings growth is a little lower than currently predicted, forecasting a 2026 earnings level of $315 (instead of the $316 analyst consensus) for the S&P 500. Between a 23x earnings multiple and a $315 profit level, we get to a 2026 S&P 500 index level of 7,245 – rounding that down again to be on the conservative side, I think that 7,200 is a reasonable target for next year. Relative to the current index level of 6880, that would mean a price increase of around 4.7%, with a dividend yield of around 1.1% on top of that. In total, my total return forecast for the broad market over the next year is thus roughly 6%. That would be a little below the long-term average, but still pretty solid and more than what one can get from most fixed-income investments today.

Takeaway

The market outlook for 2026 isn't outstanding, but far from bad. I believe: Economic growth, declining interest rates, and AI tailwinds work in investors' favor, although there are still some risks to keep an eye on, such as inflation and geopolitics.

My assumption is that the broad market will deliver a mid-single-digit return next year, which would be pretty solid, with my (possibly a little conservative) target being 7,200 for the S&P 500 at the end of 2026. Depending on how 2026 goes, this target may need adjustments throughout next year.

data.title: Palantir: When Solid Fundamentals Are Not Enough
data.description:
No Point In Buying PLTR Now

For years, I've been skeptical of Palantir's (PLTR) future, even when the stock started to rally after multiple months of deterioration in 2022. I admit that I lost the whole muti-bagger movement, not capturing the profitability shift within PLTR's business mix. But I was following pure investing logic, when one can't get sufficient returns from a stock that is priced at hundreds of years of future earnings, while showing about-average EPS growth rates along the way. The market had a contrarian view to all skeptics like me, and PLTR has been only gaining in terms of multiple contractions. The firm's fundamentals have been following suit, but in my view, their solidness has never been sufficient to justify the whole multiple expansion process.

Anyway, this discrepancy between fundamentals and valuation has widened to the point that even if we add premium growth rates on top of what's already expected, we won't see PLTR fairly valued. That's why there's no point in buying PLTR close to its ATHs now, as I see a greater chance of the stock repeating its early 2025 price action throughout 2026.

Why Do I Think So?

I don't question Palantir's fundamentals at this point, and I stopped questioning them once the firm's durable dominance in its niche became evident. For Q3 2025, the company's revenue of $1.18 billion showed a YoY growth of about 63%, where the most growth - in relative terms - came from the U.S. Commercial (+121% YoY). At this scale, the continued operating margin expansion of ~13 pts YoY, even on the non-GAAP basis, looked truly massive. It's hard to see how Palantir can jump beyond the current 51% on that metric, but specifically for Q3, the margin gains were enough to drive up the EPS growth and beat the consensus by the largest magnitude since early 2022.

Palantir's business model is getting less reliant on stock-based compensation from quarter to quarter, but much of its margin superiority depends on its SBC anyway. In Q3, the YoY growth in SBC rose again, and it's still about 14.6% of the revenue, according to YCharts data.

On an adjusted basis, the margin can be lifted insignificantly again in 2026 compared to what we've seen throughout this year, but I don't see any meaningful marginality additions that might lead to another year of extraordinary earnings expansion. The current operating leverage is here to stay, but its role fades as the base for comparison is now lifted quite high. Wall Street analysts, even following the bullish revisions after Palantir's beating in Q3, generally concur with me on the growth deterioration next year:

When sales and earnings growth rates slow down, the market is unlikely to pay the same price for the stock. At least that's my understanding. If I'm right on this front, then the repricing that I was talking about in early October might come in mid-2026, if not earlier, because the market is usually trying to price major events in advance.

Now, we're approaching the most intriguing part of any Palantir-related discussion - the valuation.

I saw many PLTR bulls arguing that the firm's superior unit economics metrics are what really justify the red-hot valuation multiples, and I partially concur with that view. Palantir's net dollar retention rate of 134% (as of Q3) and the "Rule of 40" being at 114% are really hard to match. Especially so amid the continued customer count growth.

The above "Rule of 40" is defined by Palantir as a sum of "revenue growth rate year-over-year and our adjusted operating margin for each of the periods presented", and it's a metric that is usually used by start-ups, not $450 billion+ market cap firms. Anyway, from this definition, if we keep in mind that PLTR's revenue growth rate is set to fall to high-30s vs. mid-60s to date, the company's "Rule of 40" is set to decline dramatically even if Palantir keeps its adjusted EBITDA margin at elevated levels. It's a logical consequence that comes for any high-growth entity, and 2026 is looking inevitably bad for Palantir in this regard, in my opinion. In addition to that, the NDR is also a metric that tends to be pro-cyclical in enterprise software. We don't know exactly when it can roll over, but once it does and the cycle stalls, PLTR bulls won't have an excuse for a 2027 P/E at 136x (Seeking Alpha data).

Back in late January 2025, the analysts had a consensus view that Palantir's FY2027 EPS would amount to $1.13. Now, we're approaching 2025's end, and it's at $1.42. So, assuming that by the end of 2026, PLTR's consensus FY2027 EPS grows in the same fashion (and reaches ~$1.784), the implied P/E ratio would amount to >108x. That would be a 1-year forward P/E ratio if we're talking about the late calendar 2026.

If the deterioration in growth rates comes as I expect, at some point in 2026, even the massive 25%+ premium that I put on Palantir's forward multiple won't justify the stock's price. The forward 2 years of total returns for Palantir are likely to be flat, at best, if not negative, if my math is right. But given that I can't time the exact point at which the YoY expansion stalls next year, and I'd not short PLTR like Mr. Burry, I just decided to leave my "Hold" in active mode.

The Bottom Line

My main point of concern, and the negative catalyst I'm seeing for 2026, is that the U.S. Commercial growth deterioration and the overall top-line growth deceleration are going to provoke a repricing to the downside. But if U.S. Commercial growth stays meaningfully above today's consensus for longer (not just 1-2 quarters), and Palantir's NDR remains within the 130% area rather than drifting down with tougher comps, it can make the stock's multiples more sustainable. Plus, in the Q3 quarter, we saw that Palantir's beating potential has actually gotten stronger. So, if the firm keeps executing the way it did throughout 2025, and the high bar they've already reached doesn't bother to show another stunning year of excellent growth, the likely outcome would be another rally for PLTR bulls. And if the premium that I put on PLTR's forward EPS turns out to be insufficient, the stock can adjust higher quite easily, as the demand for the stock is very high (especially on any dip, even minor ones).

Anyway, I see the incremental upside from margin to be smaller than bullishly minded people think to date, and the current growth stage is very likely to face a harder comparison environment next year. The fundamentals of the business look solid, and I don't question that part at all. But everything has its natural limits. The price action that Palantir is going through today reminds any historical bubble that can pop before the underlying bubble on the market really forms. At 136x FY2027 earnings, PLTR is too hot to touch when we see its forward YoY growth rates.

I can't and don't want to time the market, so I'm just staying on the sidelines, forecasting a harder environment for PLTR in 2026.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Lundin Mining: I Believe Rotation Should Be Considered
data.description:
Note: All dollar amounts are in Canadian Dollars unless otherwise stated

I started coverage of Lundin Mining (LUN: CA) this year at what I believed was a conservative, attractive valuation. Then the company rose triple digits, defying every valuation trend and growth trend known, and we find our update with the company trading at a Canadian dollar value close to $29/share for the native Canadian ticker, LUN.

I argue that this trend, based on company volatility and the underlying market, was not have been forecasted in any situation. It defied the company's trading history in every way, and it goes to show you that no one can really predict with 100% forecast accuracy where a company goes. It has outperformed most tech stocks, most AI stocks in 6 months (a few are up 100%+ after all), and we're talking about a conservative copper miner of all things.

Take a look at this graph and see if you believe that this trend has anything solid supporting it, apart from near-term trends.

As I made abundantly clear in my last article, found here, this a qualitative mining company with plenty of operational upside at the right valuation. As you can also see, if bought at the wrong time, say before GFC, you can enjoy negative returns (because the company had not dividend at the time, and did not have one for many, many years), for over a decade before the company recovers. This company's trajectory and history are anything but smooth.

The same can be said for its forecast accuracy, because there is almost none. More than 90% of the time, the company misses its forecast, reflecting the underlying volatile trends of the market.

Had I been able to forecast this trend, I would have, of course, invested heavily - but my point is not that you should find every undervalued Copper miner on the market and buy them here.

Rather, my point is that you should consider rotating this one for profit before it drops down again.

Let me show you why.

Lundin Mining - Upside is very limited here.

So, as mentioned, Lundin isn't your typical company that should "surge" as much as it has in the short term.

So why has it, exactly?

A mix of reasons.

The most important of it is that base metals, such as Copper, have been seeing significant upticks in demand and thus, price, for a few months now. This has outperformed even the increased cost trends that the company has shown us. In fact, my recent article on Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) could be argued to be based on the same trends as we're seeing in Lundin, albeit from different metal trends.

Yield for this company is sub-par. We're talking 0.4%. While copper companies usually aren't yield monsters, there are many copper companies, both more conservative and with bigger market capitalizations, that offer better yields than Lundin.

The combination of a stronger production guidance, coupled with demand, coupled with tariffs, coupled with earnings (based on metals income), coupled with overall lower production costs (now below $2.01/lb in any case), is what's driving the company up here, even though these production efficiencies primarily come from sites like Caserones and the Chapada asset. It's been divesting EU-based assets, reflecting its focus on fewer continental assets with lower margins, to instead focus on higher-risk, but significantly higher-margin assets.

The proceeds from these assets have gone to shore up an already-impressive balance sheet. But given its focus on higher-risk assets, its operations as mostly debt-free is an absolute necessity.

The company also has a couple of high-growth projects, which are considered impressive prospects. Lundin does mostly copper, but it also has exposure to gold and silver - especially in these higher-growth projects. Another explanation for the company's upside is how excellent gold has been doing, and the increased Lundin exposure to this metal with its new growth projects.

The gold upside, silver upside, coupled with increased copper demand (and the subsequent market rating of many of these copper businesses) is what's responsible for the upside here.

The primary worry I have about Lundin is that its previous valuation and safety trends were underpinned by significant operational and legacy safety based on EU-located assets. I'm talking about the same assets that Lundin Mining has now divested in favor of higher-growth assets.

You can see that this is what the company goes for. It's arguing for stable copper production (likely), with a world-class partner (also likely, the company has low debt), with transformative growth projects (work as well), with a proven execution track record (yep, confirmed).

The problem with all of the above is that they're based on the assumption of spot pricing for their metals, and that this is to remain stable. And do you know why people are cautious about investing in volatile commodities like metals? Because they are the opposite of stable. The reason for the volatility is severalfold.

It includes everything from energy pricing to geopolitical instability to FX, to financial speculation to significant shifts in supply and demand. While the company's moves have lowered risks in terms of energy costs, the company is at the same time seeing higher risks of geopolitical instability, FX, and the like, because of its rising main geographies.

I think the biggest mistake an investor could make after a surge like this of over 100% is to invest money in the hope of another surge. The company is already trading almost 100% higher than its historical average, and while current forecasts seem to look rosy (2026-2027), there are plenty of reasons why copper will decline in the medium term, and drag Lundin down alongside it.

To understand this, why is copper up anyway?

Copper is up due to the demand based on the energy transition. That means that an impact based on the energy transition or larger economic trend - especially something like a China slowdown- could easily impact copper demand. But things like higher interest rates, more trade tensions, and the like could also impact copper pricing. Also, realize that many copper assets are found in volatile geographies. In seeing the demand for their assets, regulators in those areas may impose stricter policies, leading to higher costs, project delays, political risks, and the like.

There's a reason why a company like this tends to miss estimates more than 80-90% of the time, and none of those reasons are positive.

The current surge in valuation is related only somewhat to Lundin Mining's operational excellence. Its strategic moves only have a small connection to what's happening here. More than anything, it's a combination of commodity pricing/spot pricing and exuberance. The same sort of exuberance that's driving the market in general right now.

More than anything, this trend is proof to me that the time to invest in commodities is when those commodities are at the bottom - and then hold with patience for an upside like this.

But I believe there is no better time to leave this company and to take profits at this time.

Here is why.

Valuation and upside for Lundin Mining - Time to take profits.

As I've said in other articles, I reserve my "SELL" ratings for companies with fundamental issues or safety concerns. That's not the case here. Lundin Mining is a materially qualitative mining business with close to zero debt and good upside, if somewhat volatile asset geographies.

Its problem is valuation. Lundin is almost twice its 20-year average, and despite an EPS surge in the next 2 years, I don't believe that A), that surge will last, and B) that valuation is justified here even for the long term. For example, I point you to the copper volatility of 2021-2022, followed by the 2022-2023 crash, when Lundin fell to 6-7x P/E, compared to 30x P/E.

The fact that the estimated rate of annualized return from here on is negative 20%+ until 2027E, based on a growth rate of double digits here, is dangerous.

How do I come up with this?

I use the 5-year average P/E, which is significantly higher than the 20-year average. I forecast this based on current estimated growth rates, and we arrive at an annualized return of negative 12% even with the yield, and a share price of $21/share.

Even if you were to forecast Lundin Mining at something like a 30x P/E here, you'd only get 11% per year out of that based on these surge-like forecasts. That is not a good rate of return.

Peers to the company are many, and many have similar types of focus. I'll go through a couple of them here. Capstone Copper (CS:CA) is a pure-play copper and also with the same sort of volatility and trend. I'd say "no" here, unless you're willing to take a significant risk following almost 15 years of underperformance. Freeport (FCX) is another pure-play copper with a native US listing, but its trends are almost identical to Lundin. All in all, the entire sector shows us its overvalued face here, as well as the fact that outside of this overvaluation, it's a volatile, prone-to-misses sort of place with significant risk.

I do not view any of these peers as appealingly valued or attractive investments here. The upside in copper has played itself out, in my view, and the only thing remaining here is either negative RoR or risk.

On the basis of this, I review my PT and fair value target for Lundin. The previous target was $15.5/share. I'd allow a 17-18x P/E upside for this company, discounting for the company's lack of forecast accuracy, while allowing higher growth than companies tied to more legacy-type assets in more conservative Geographies. A $20-$21/share fair value is possible, which would make a PT of about $16.5/share worthy of an upside, representing a slight bump from my last article.

Other analysts have the company at a low of about $14.2, to a high of about $23/share, with an average of $20.19 - so about the same fair value as I have, perhaps a bit higher. Given that 11 out of 22 analysts are at "BUY", the ratings are somewhat split - but I would argue there's an insufficient amount of caution here given the levels the company is currently trading at, relative to targets (and not a single analyst being above $23.28/share.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

My "HOLD" rating for Lundin Mining has the risk of the company outperforming averages, or the stock outperforming as it has in the past. The company's asset sales from an international perspective have given the company stability that could result in higher returns and results. If this were to happen, the price target needs to go up. The continued JV's could also increase the company's counter-cyclical resilience, and this is hard to estimate.

However, I believe the company is better suited for a "HOLD" here, especially given the latest trends and what could be expected from the lack of upside following this surge.

Thesis

Lundin Mining is a complex mining company with a solid long-term upside, owned 15%+ by the billionaire Lundin Family out of Sweden. The company operates mining assets across the world, with a focus on South America, which represents more than 80% of revenue and profit.

I believe the company is likely to be one of the major copper players on a forward basis, and I believe, at the right value, the company is worth investing in. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of $20/share, which in turn comes to a $16.5/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a PT of $16.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "HOLD" rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Still Undervalued And Generating 4.65% Dividend Yield
data.description:
It’s been a great end of the year for Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) as shares have rallied over 27% since October from $42.52 to $54.19. I believe that BMY is still a misunderstood company as its been looked at as a value trap for several years. The investment community seems to be more focused on what is rolling off patent than what is ramping up. After going through the Q3 results I think this narrative is incorrect and BMY looks less like a company that is in a managed decline and more like a company in transition as its growing its slate of newer brands and late-stage assets. Over time I believe that BMY can offset the revenue loss from assets coming off patent which should help the market change its narrative. The reality is that BMY is generating over $15 billion in free cash flow (FCF), has strong financials, is back to profitability, and growing the dividend. BMY still trades at a single digit P/E with a dividend yield of 4.65%. I am very bullish on BMY as I feel it’s an overlooked opportunity for both yield and capital appreciation. As the risk-free rate of return declines BMY’s growing dividend is likely to look more appealing and the lower Fed Effective Rate could create a situation where BMY is able to expand margins by issuing new debt to pay off higher yielding existing debt. It’s been a disappointing decade for BMY shareholders but if the broad market rallies in 2026 BMY is certainly a candidate that can capitalize on that opportunity.

Following up on my previous article about Bristol Myers

At the beginning of August I wrote an article about BMY (can be read here) discussing why I felt it was undervalued. BMY delivered a double beat on their Q2 earnings and raised their year end guidance as they continued to execute a successful transition from legacy drugs to a growth portfolio. I believed that BMY added several strategic partnerships in Q2 which made it a contrarian value and income play. I am following up with a new article because I still believe that the market is discounting BMY’s future cash flow. BMY is trading at 8.29 times 2025 earnings and paying a dividend yield of 4.65%. BMY is generating over $15 billion in annualized FCF and has a strong enough balance sheet to continue transitioning toward prospective growth projects. I think that BMY will continue to deliver strong dividend growth and capital appreciation for long-term investors.

BMY’s Q3 results solidified their transition to future growth

In Q3 BMY delivered $12.22 billion in revenue which was an increase of 3% YoY while beating the consensus estimates by $422.1 million. BMY was able to generate $1.63 of EPS which came in $0.11 higher than what The Street was looking for. Q3 showcased to the market that all the investments that BMY has made is paying off. It’s growth portfolio generated $6.9 billion in revenue which was an increase of 18% YoY while the legacy drug portfolio declined -12% YoY to $5.4 billion in revenue. The strength from the growth portfolio was enough to offset the declining revenue from its previous drugs while allowing BMY to increase its 2025 projections to roughly $47.5–$48.0 billion of revenue while guiding for non-GAAP EPS between $6.40–$6.60. BMY is showing the market that it is able to build future durable income through its diversified growth portfolio. The theme in Q3 wasn’t that growth was outperforming the legacy portfolio even if that’s the way it looked on the surface. BMY has multiple products that are compounding at the same time which reduces dependency on one product which improves and diversifies its revenue mix.

When I look at the oncology portfolio it remained a cornerstone for BMY generating $2.53 billion which was up 7% YoY. This was aided by momentum in Yervoy which produced $739 million in revenue up 15% YoY and Opdualag which produced $299 million in revenue which was up 28% YoY. BMY’s oncology segment isn’t focused on one product as their working on several regimens, combinations, and indications that can fortify its revenue mix in an environment where competition is getting more fierce. BMY watched Reblozyl’s revenue increase 37% YoY to $615 million while Breyanzi increased 60% YoY to $359 million. While the market is looking at BMY as a company that is in transition I am seeing them as a company that is scaling several compounds from their infancy to being real contributors to the top and bottom line.

I believe that BMY is delivering on its strategy of accelerating its growth portfolio, diversifying its pipeline through business development, and maintaining balance sheet flexibility. BMY has leaned into strategic transactions to diversify future growth drivers. In October 2025 BMY entered into an agreement to acquire Orbital Therapeutics for $1.5 billion in cash which provided diversification into next generation platforms which included RNA enables compounds. BMY also entered into a partnership with BioNTech where BMY gained access to an oncology asset BNT327. This has allowed BMY’s revenue mix to be fortified by the growth portfolio as the strategic pivot in BMY’s product mix includes oncology, cardiovascular, immunology. and neuroscience. BMY finished Q3 with $16.5 billion in cash and short-term investments while having paid more than $5 billion of its long-term debt off since Q1 2024. BMY has the flexibility in its balance sheet and cash flow to continue raising the dividend and buying back stock from the $5 billion left on the authorization.

I believe that BMY has positioned themselves for the future as they have constructed the growth portfolio to have enough clinical catalysts to manage the legacy portfolios decline. This is a step to returning toward sticky growth as the growth portfolio growing by 18% YoY has transcended incremental. BMY is also entering into a data rich period with multiple catalysts across CNS, immunology, cardiovascular, hematology, and oncology. The market could rerate BMY if these products can become ongoing revenue streams. BMY flagged Cobenfy Alzheimer’s Disease Psychosis and Cobenfy Bipolar-1 as major upcoming catalysts. CNS can help unlock value for BMY because it could expand BMY’s footprint. BMY could also recognize a lot of value in immunology through Sotyktu. If BMY experiences successful expansions it would transform Sotyktu from a single-indication growth asset into a broader immunology franchise. BMY has the ability to get rerated if several of these milestones turn into category drivers.

I still see a lot of value in shares of BMY

CME Group is projecting that we are going to get 2 rate cuts in 2026 taking the target rate down to 300-325 bps. BMY is paying a dividend of $2.52 per share which is a 4.65% yield in an environment where the risk-free rate of return is declining. BMY has been able to increased the dividend for 17 years with an average growth rate of 6.62% over the past 5-years. BMY has a 37.80% payout ratio and I believe they will continue increasing the dividend considering they generate in excess of $15 billion in FCF annually. As rates continue to fall BMY’s dividend yield will look more and more enticing and I believe that locking in the yield here will be a beneficial for investors going forward.

I compared BMY to Pfizer (PFE), GSK plc (GSK), Sanofi (SNY), and Merck & CO (MRK) and when I looked at the numbers BMY looks very undervalued. I looked at price to EBITDA, operating income, cash from operations, FCF, and net income to determine if BMY is expensive or not. Over the TTM BMY has generated $19.22 billion in EBITDA, $14.32 billion in operating income, $16.62 billion in cash from ops, $15.3 billion in FCF, and $6 billion in net income. When I compare the multiples on each of these metrics BMY has the lowest valuation on 4 of these 5 metrics. BMY trades at 5.74 times EBITDA, 7.71 times operating income, 6.64 times operating income, and 7.21 times FCF. None of its peers that I listed trade at a multiple of less than 10 times FCF. I think that the level of profitability that BMY generates is being discounted and there is an opportunity for unlocking value at these levels.

Risks to investing in BMY

While I am bullish on BMY there are several risk factors to consider. BMY has a legacy portfolio that is eroding in market share. With declining revenue from its legacy products the market is likely to be skeptical if the growth side of its business will be able to offset a continuation in losses from products coming off patent. BMY also has a risk in pricing due to the political scrutiny about Medicare. If reimbursement levels change then BMY could experience compressed margins and less profitability in the future. There is also no guarantee that its pipeline of prospective products will make it out of clinical trials. For every product that fails it’s a loss of not just invested capital but time as well. Pharmaceuticals are difficult because anything can happen in the clinical trials that can derail long-term projects. Investors should do their own due diligence as investing in BMY comes with many large risks.

Conclusion

BMY is undergoing a transition where its growth portfolio is starting to scale and outpace its declining legacy portfolio. I believe that the legacy portfolio will continue to scale and allow BMY to increase its profitability over time making the current valuations look inexpensive. If this occurs the market could rerate shares of BMY as they would see less risk from drugs coming off patent. BMY trading at less than 8 times FCF, cash from operations, operating income, and EBITDA is a valuation that I am not passing on. Investors are able to lock in a 4.6% dividend yield at these levels while getting BMY at an inexpensive valuation. Over the next year I believe that BMY will look more like a growth story than a legacy drug maker on the decline and investors may be able to unlock a lot of value at these levels while getting paid a large dividend at a time when the risk-free rate of return is declining.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Where The Risk Of Overbuilding Meets The Road
data.description:
Summary

Meta Platforms (META) is in the midst of a transformative investment cycle. Capital expenditures in 2025 are now expected to be around $70 billion, a sharp increase from the $37.2 billion reported last year.

Meta's strategy has been to front-load its AI infrastructure buildout to ensure that it does not miss any opportunities. While this corporate strategy could yield tremendous benefits if superintelligence were to happen sooner rather than later, it also exposes the company to the risk of overbuilding in the meantime.

Given the stretched valuation multiples and the hundreds of billions worth of capital that have flowed into the AI industry, I remain cautious not only about Meta but on the industry as a whole.

An Aggressive AI Infrastructure Buildout

After researching Blue Owl Capital (OWL), I now turn my attention to Meta Platforms, another key player in the AI infrastructure boom. Following their $27 billion data center financing deal announced in October 2025, there is a close relationship between the two companies. The planned data center campus in rural Louisiana, known as Hyperion, could scale up to 5GW of computing power, making it one of the world's largest data centers. Hyperion's site spans over a massive 2,250 acres of land, or the equivalent of a significant portion of lower and mid-town Manhattan, as illustrated below.

To put the scale of what is about to become Meta's largest data center, 5GW is roughly twice what the city of New Orleans consumes in electricity on a peak day. Entergy (ETR), is now seeking regulatory approval for the construction of three natural gas-fired power plants that could cost upward of $3.0 billion. With surging electricity demand in the United States, it is as if the energy transition towards renewable sources has taken a back seat. More specifically, electric utilities in the United States have delayed the retirement of 30 generating units at 15 power plants due to surging electricity demand from data centers, and Dominion Energy's (D) coal-fired Clover power station in Virginia is a great example of that.

According to a January 2025 report from the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis, we find that electric utilities in Virginia,North Carolina, South Carolina and Georgia are planning to build 20 GW of gas-fired power plants by 2040. In the meantime, pipeline operators in the region are also planning to bolster capacity by more than 3,300 million cubicfeet a day. The report mentions that this significant energy infrastructure buildout is directly linked to surging demand from data centers and AI applications.

The reason why I mention all of those details is to illustrate the scale of the ongoing AI infrastructure boom, and also the technical and operational risks that come with it. Being one of the most aggressive players in this historic construction boom, Meta's business model is not only changing but also becoming riskier over time.

Surging Capital Intensity

In this context, it should not come as a surprise that Meta's capital intensity has increased meaningfully over the last few years, and it is only expected to continue in 2026. As discussed in the Q3-25 conference call, Meta is aggressively front-loading its AI infrastructure build-out to ensure that it has a leading market position in case artificial general intelligence (AGI) or artificial superintelligence (ASI) is achieved sooner than expected. If we go a step further, we could even talk about artificial consciousness, a theoretical concept in which machines become self-aware and able to experience emotions, similar to humans. Even though Zuckerberg did not use those precise terms, it is a if there is some fear of missing out in Meta's corporate strategy, and in my view, this is precisely the kind of dynamic that could lead to an overbuilding of data center capacity.

''There is a range of timelines for when people think that we are going to get superintelligence. Some people think that we will get there in a few years, others think it will be 5 years, 7 years, or longer. I think it is the right strategy to aggressively front-load building capacity so that way we are prepared for the most optimistic cases. That way, if superintelligence arrives sooner, we will be ideally positioned for a generational paradigm shift and many large opportunities.''

Even though Hyperion is not expected to be fully completed until 2030, Meta is about to bring online sometime in 2026 its massive 1GW Prometheus data center located in New Albany, Ohio. Once completed, Prometheus will be one of the world's largest AI data center campuses, although it may not remain so for very long, considering the speed of developments within the industry. The large computing power difference between the 1GW Prometheus and the 5GW Hyperion clusters also illustrated how the AI infrastructure buildout is about to scale over the next few years. When I think about the size and scope of those projects, I can only think about the required capital expenditures to bring those plans to fruition.

In the third quarter of 2025, Meta reported capital expenditures of $19.4 billion, the equivalent of a ~110% increase versus the prior year's number of $9.2 billion. On a year-to-date basis, the picture is pretty much the same, as capital expenditures pretty much doubled from $24.4 billion in 2024 to $50.1 billion in 2025. As indicated in the Q3-25 press release, Meta also increased the lower end of its 2025 CapEx guidance, from $66 billion to $70 billion. More importantly, we can also read that 'capital expenditures dollar growth will be notably larger in 2026 than in 2025'.

If I draw parallels with my previous research on Oracle (ORCL), I see that capital expenditures for both companies increased significantly over the last few years, but that it is only a start. As a reminder, Oracle's 2025 CapEx guidance was increased from the previous $35 billion to the current $50 billion. This number is expected to grow even larger in 2026, before peaking only in 2028. On a combined basis, that means that Oracle and Meta are expected to deploy ~$130 billion worth of capital in 2025 alone.

Of note, Microsoft (MSFT) also exceeded its quarterly CapEx guidance of $30 billion after reporting capital spending of $34.9 billion in the first quarter of its fiscal 2026. On an annualized basis, MSFT's capital expenditures amount to ~$140 billion, roughly equivalent to the combined capital spending of Oracle and Meta. In other words, the challenges associated with increasing capital intensity are not unique to Meta but are widespread across the AI ecosystem.

Financial Overview

In the third quarter of 2025, Meta reported operating cash flows of $30 billion relative to capital expenditures of $18.8 billion. With free cash flows of $11.2 billion during the quarter, Meta still has quite a bit of room to cover future increases in capital spending. In my view, this is one of the key differences between Meta and Oracle, in the sense that ORCL's free cash flows are already deeply in the red despite the fact that capital spending is still ramping up.

Even though it does not address the risk of overbuilding, Meta's capital spending toward AI infrastructure is at least underpinned by a highly cash-generative advertising business operating at scale, given its 3.54 billion daily active users at the end of September 2025. With a 'AA-' credit rating at S&P, Meta also has solid access to capital markets, as evidenced by the company's $30 billion bond issuance announced at the end of October 2025, its largest ever. The last time Meta tapped the bond market was in August 2022, when it issued $10 billion worth of debt to support its metaverse initiative.

As illustrated below, Meta's balance sheet remains in a pristine condition, with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities totalling $44.4 billion and long-term debt of only $28.8 billion. The company's net cash balance declined meaningfully over the last few quarters, from $49.0 billion at the end of December 2024 to $15.6 billion at the end of September 2025. Keeping in mind Meta's CapEx growth trajectory, it is to wonder if the company will enter net debt territory for the first time since its IPO in May 2012.

Valuation

As mentioned in the section above, Meta generated free cash flows of $11.2 billion in the third quarter of 2025, or the equivalent of $44.8 billion on an annualized basis. Based on the current $1.66 trillion market capitalization, we are then talking about a free cash flow yield of ~2.7%. If we look over the last decade, we can see that Meta's current free cash flow yield is at the lower end of its historical range. Given the expected increase in capital expenditures next year, I would expect free cash flows to remain compressed over the foreseeable future.

In addition to the numerous execution risks associated with its historic infrastructure buildout, Meta currently trades at a stretched free cash flow multiple, which adds even more downside risks. Given the company's strategy to front-load capital deployments toward AI infrastructure, I view Meta as particularly vulnerable to the risk of overbuilding computing capacity.

Risk Factor

There are multiple risks associated with my 'Sell' rating. First of all, the ongoing AI infrastructure boom could last much longer than anything I can expect. Moreover, in contrast to neocloud providers like CoreWeave (CRWV), Nebius (NBIS) and IREN (IREN) or smaller hyperscalers like Oracle, Meta's capital deployments are underpinned by a strong balance sheet in a net cash position, and a highly cash-generative advertising business operating at scale. Even if the current euphoria surrounding AI infrastructure turns into fear, Meta's share price could still outperform many of its peers due to those factors.

Conclusion

With hundreds of billions flowing into the AI infrastructure business, I remain concerned about the risk of overbuilding. Needless to say that it would have dire consequences for returns on invested capital.

Meta's capital expenditures increased significantly over the last few years and are expected to continue so over the short and medium term. For example, management increased the lower end of the 2025 CapEx guidance range to $70 billion while preparing the analyst community for another large CapEx increase in 2026.

With surging capital expenditures, Meta's free cash flows have been under pressure, and the stock is now trading at the lower end of its historical free cash flow yield. This means that on top of the execution risks associated with a massive infrastructure buildout, shareholders must also assume some valuation risk. History is ripe with examples in which the euphoria of a historic construction boom turned into panic, followed by a prolonged recessionary period.

The North American canal construction boom of the early 1800s, the railroad mania of the late 1800s or the dot-com bubble of the early 2000s are mere examples of the same cycle of boom and bust observable throughout history. In each case, a technological revolution was indeed taking place, and the world indeed changed forever afterwards. However, that did not change the fact that in the midst of the euphoria, an overbuilding of infrastructure fuelled by cheap credit was taking place.

The personal computer combined with the internet changed the world in ways that nobody could have forecasted in the late 1990s and early 2000s. The same thing could happen with AI, but as history shows, that does not mean that a bust cannot happen. It is from this perspective that I view Meta as a 'Sell'.

data.title: JD.com: The Financial Fortress Powering Asia's E-Commerce Boom At A Deep Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered JD.com (JD), I reiterated their Strong Buy rating, supported by continued strong growth and user momentum as they’re leveraging their excellent financial position to expand both locally and internationally.

Now, with the stock down ~19% since the last analysis and the company’s latest report beating the market’s expectations at a time when macro factors are taking an interesting turn, I believe the opportunity is even better, with JD still trading well below even a more conservative fair value estimate.

Internal Developments

JD’s Q3 report was very solid overall, beating the market’s expectations with a 11.4% YoY increase in net revenue, although the free cash flow was affected from several points of view. First, we’re talking about massive jumps in marketing expenses due to their “war” with Meituan (MPNGF) for the food delivery market (hurting cash flows from operations), while CAPEX - despite being reduced - were still high due their infrastructure investments, with working capital also affected by inventory build-ups. Overall, JD’s CFO summarized the quarter quite well from an internal point of view during the Q3 Earnings Call:

In summary, we're encouraged by the solid progress in both core retail and new business. Retail has built a growth metric with multiple drivers and a clear path to our long-term margin target. Food delivery is growing with a healthier financial model and other new business, including lower tier market and international business are also making solid steps to the next chapter. All our businesses are on the right track, starting to generate notable synergies with 1 another and collectively contributing to our high-quality development in the long term.

Recently, JD’s supply-chain technology unit (Jingdong Industrials) announced it's exploring a potential HK IPO that may raise around $500 million (raised ~$383.08 million), which comes after they initially filed for an IPO back in March 2023 but only received the approval in September 2025.

Meanwhile, JD announced it will acquire a 50% stake in Hong Kong’s CCB Tower for ~$450 million, adding a 27-story office tower together with a unit of China Construction Bank. This is an interesting move seen from players like Alibaba (BABA) recently, basically doing some “bargain hunting” in HK’s office real estate market, which crashed ~40-50% from its 2018 peaks while also improving their ties with various state-owned entities and developing their HK presence that can serve as a bridge for their international expansion.

Financially, based on JD’s latest report, we see an excellent financial position, with the current assets nearly covering their total liabilities. Something we’ve seen for Baidu (BIDU) as well is the very significant amount of cash and marketable securities (current and long-term), with JD coming with about $28.18 billion in net cash if we include long-term marketable securities on a $40.90 billion market cap, which can signal a very significant discount for the company’s actual operations.

Meanwhile, JD continues to pay a solid ~3.47% dividend yield combined with roughly $1.5 billion in buybacks during the first 9 months of 2025, implying an annualized buyback yield of 4.9%, with $3.5 billion left in their $5 billion buyback program until August 2027.

Macro Developments

Similar to Baidu - which I covered recently in this article - and other Chinese assets, one factor that could attract a potential re-pricing could be a potential attack on Venezuela. I’m assuming everyone is up to date with this, although it remains a rapidly-developing matter with news that affect the market coming almost daily now. The most recent development comes from the US intercepting another oil tanker off Venezuela’s coast, with tensions rising in an already tense environment.

Again, this can actually be a positive for Chinese stocks because of its effects on global trade and especially due to its effects on the market’s opinions. The “winner” of the war itself is quite clear and I doubt anyone is debating that, but a conflict in the Caribbean can affect the Panama Canal due to its proximity and importance, potentially causing spikes in oil prices combined with disruptions for the region’s general trade with the US, affecting trading routes, supply chains, travel, cause delays, etc.

Not only that, but as Brazil’s president said recently, this can create “a dangerous precedent for the world,” which would only accentuate the already visible weakening in the market’s opinion regarding the safety of investing in the US, on top of tariffs and many other things that already caused precious metal prices to skyrocket like we’ve never seen in history.

This has the potential to push “big money” into realizing the importance of hedging with global assets - similar to how it woke up to the need for precious metals recently. As such, the market may not be willing to fund what became an unpredictable nation during a war so close to home for an only ~4% yield, potentially boosting the demand for international assets. If Trump names what the market would consider an inadequate Fed Chair soon (Powell’s term expires in May 2026), that can only reinforce this risk.

Something I mentioned for Baidu recently was the with re-rising tensions between the US and China, especially after the Pentagon talked about the inclusion of companies like Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD (BYDDF) to the list of Chinese military companies. Although it’s not really that big in this regard, this move can reignite fears around the de-listing/regulatory block of Chinese ADRs, which would cut off a significant amount of demand.

But again, this kind of risk already seems to be priced in as part of the “China discount” to some degree, being a possibility we should always keep in mind when investing in China.

Still, over the long-term JD will likely continue its focus on being a supply chain backbone for the booming trade between China and SEA or even beyond. This comes as JD recently accelerated their SEA supply chain investments, with the region being probably the most attractive from a long-term growth point of view, with cross-border e-commerce in the SEA region being expected to grow at a very significant ~11.14% CAGR through 2030, with JD potentially being there to make the logistics behind this possible.
